tweets.text:
What if we measure how good AI models are by what era of games it can one shot a single level for (or maybe build with 30 min of work):

Atari
NES
SNES, Sega Genesis
PlayStation, N64
PS2, Xbox, GameCube, Dreamcast
Xbox 360, PS3, Wii
PS4, Xbox One, Switch
PS5, Xbox Series X/S

I think we're still in the NES era?

Solo AI founders tend to get stuck trying to make one product work. Here's @tibo_maker advice on what you should do instead:

"We had this crazy phase where we were shipping one new product a week until something stuck.

We were just printing the landing page, tweeting about it, and if there's no traction, we go to the next product.

Nine products failed that way and the 10th one just took off.

I would not have bet on my successful products in advance. They were a surprise to me too.

I think you need to change your mindset and see every product release as an experiment. It can totally fail, you just go to the next experiment and continue."

tl:dr Ship early and often, don't try to make it perfect.

📌 My full interview with Tibo is full of practical advice for bootstrapping AI products: https://t.co/ty2bTCUSyk

builders.name: Aaron Levie
builders.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

tweets.text:
Agentic coding is a huge boon for software developers that want to get far more done, great for IT people to build vastly more custom systems internally, great for domain experts that want to automate workflows or wire systems together, and absolutely fantastic for anyone curious to learn how to start coding. 

What it’s less great for is casually building complex software that you have to maintain on an ongoing basis and take on all the risk for. Upgrades, maintenance, keeping up to date with latest security issues, and so on, are taxes most knowledge workers aren’t familiar with or prepared for.

Net net: we’re going to get 100X more software and vastly more software developers in the future. But that’s different from *everyone* rolling their own.
https://t.co/syK3QYG52A

tweets.text:
using posthog inside of codex

it is SO MUCH better to browse posthog inside of codex—codex can write queries and see the results, and it makes our collaboration extremely seamless. codex can also kick off agents that write PRs or run production DB requests to back up insights it's finding

a browser inside your desktop coding orchestration tool > an agent in your browser

we might need to coin a new term:

Codex-native
Cowork-native
Cursor-native

apps designed to be used inside of the in-app browser of your agent of choice. the agent can use it and you can use it too, full context is shared, and you can both see what the other is doing.

huge opportunity to build software here

tweets.text: bitcoin ($BTC) at $76k with 16m+ BTC locked in long-term holders, corporate treasuries, and sovereign wallets. that leaves ~5m BTC for actual price discovery across every exchange on earth. strategy alone is absorbing 1,229 BTC per day, 2x the daily mining output. if the U.S. formalizes 328k seized BTC as strategic reserves in may, those coins go from "might sell" to "never sell." 23 nations now running sovereign bitcoin strategies targeting 2.4m BTC combined. 40% below ATH during the most aggressive supply removal in bitcoin's 17-year history. the bear case is 1 billion. believe in something.

morpho ($MORPHO) absorbed $8b from aave in 72 hours after the kelp exploit. 90% of active loans are stablecoins. aave's response to losing 44% of its TVL is redesigning V4 with isolated markets. they're copying morpho's architecture. that's not competition, that's validation. morpho midnight launched fixed-rate fixed-term lending the same day kelp blew up. ready, steakhouse, and coinbase all integrated within the same month. the protocol mitigated $1m in losses by proactively pausing vaults before contagion spread. aave ate $230m in bad debt. when your biggest competitor has to rebuild their entire stack to look like you, capital already made its choice.

tweets.text: I changed my mind on how AI will impact jobs in America… https://t.co/wbywsTOy5E

I keep seeing survey data about how much people hate AI.

It may be counterintuitive, but this is a very positive sign for the AI industry. In order to understand why, you have to realize that a portion of the population has built a fake religion around opposing any form of progress.

They hate capitalism. They hate free speech. They hate Amazon, Google, Facebook, and the litany of other companies that have drastically improved our lives.

So when these people report hating AI, they are actually telling on themselves. They are implicitly admitting that AI is valuable and will create enormous economic prosperity in the world.

Because AI will do this, it has to be opposed by the people who have tied their identify to anti-progress and anti-innovation.

The wrong conclusion to take from these surveys would be “AI is going to run into too much opposition.” Instead, you should gain confidence that the free market is going to adopt the best technology.

And that technology is very clearly artificial intelligence now.
Good morning. Today is going to be a great day. Let’s get after it relentlessly.

> the new managed agent's product = enterprise chatgpt agents for all of amazon's clients. openai might just snatch back the enterprise market share they lost

i'll keep beating the drum, openai and amazon are undergoing a huge narrative shift.

massive earnings day tmrw - google, amazon, microsoft, meta and apple are spending $600B+ on AI this year, tmrw we find out if its worth it,

some indicators over the last 7 days:

- openai is back. codex isn't good, its fucking great and developers are switching over.

- consensus: gpt 5.5 > opus 4.7

- "openai missed revenue target" this is a LAGGING indicator. codex wasn't good back then, gpt 5.4 wasn't competitive. now they're both #1

- microsoft came out winning in the openai break-up. they take 20% of openai rev, can support competitors on azure and don't have to pay openai azure rev. they also own 27% of openai

- amazon in undergoing a narrative shift, people are waking up to the fact they're #1 distributor of models (i expect to see this reflected tmrw)

- google investing up to $40B in anthropic + the money comes back to them in compute rev = great.

- meta's going to unwind the manus deal which might disrupt their ai advertising lead over google (but that won't show till q2)

my guess: ai cloud revenue is going to CRUSH. amazon, google and microsoft beat expectations.

tweets.text:
#BTC

Bitcoin has been able to press beyond both old All Time Highs from 2021 and 2024

That said, there are technical nuances to this ascent

For example, Bitcoin has been able to successfully turn the 2021 old All Time Highs into support on the Weekly timeframe (green circle)

However, Bitcoin hasn't necessarily performed a standard retest of the 2024 old All Time Highs (orange circle)

Should Bitcoin dip more then that would be a logical retesting area in the short-term

And should Bitcoin indeed Monthly Close above both of these old ATHs, then a retest of both of these levels would be on the cards

$BTC #Crypto #Bitcoin

#BTC 

At the moment, the 21-week EMA (green) is acting more as resistance than a support that price is trying to retest

The retest was always going to be difficult given the marginal Weekly Close but it's still technically a possibility

However, once the EMA starts to firmly act as resistance, then that post-breakout retest of the top of the Double Bottom formation (blue $72k) would once again be in play

$BTC #Crypto #Bitcoin

tweets.text:
Stablecoins flipped between crisis and credibility in the span of 72 hours.

Here’s what happened.

→ On April 22, Justin Sun filed a lawsuit against World Liberty Financial, pulling political and crypto audiences into the same narrative loop. Eric Trump stepped in publicly within hours.

→ On April 23, Tether froze 344M USDT for law enforcement. The headline went viral, hitting 1.28M impressions on a single post.

→ At the same time, the 292M KelpDAO / rsETH exploit continued cascading through Aave and DeFi liquidity pools, forcing emergency responses across the ecosystem.

3 separate shocks. One combined effect: A crisis-driven narrative that took over stablecoin mindshare mid-week.

The fallout didn’t stay contained. Weekly signal dropped -31% WoW. Impressions fell -20%, and Unique authors down -11%.

But this wasn’t disengagement. It was post-spike normalization after a high-intensity news cycle.

What the CT conversation actually looks like.

Neutral posts dominated volume (~89%), but high-signal engagement skewed bearish, concentrated around the exploit and the Tether freeze.

2 sides of the same market.

“Stablecoins are fragile”
Exploits, freezes, lawsuits → systemic risk concerns

“Stablecoins are going mainstream”
Institutions, governments, enforcement → proof of relevance
Both gained traction at the same time.

What got overlooked.

Gemini launched agentic trading, enabling AI models to execute trades directly using stablecoins.
